frontmatter.title: The formal theory of relative monads

mainmatter.p: We develop the theory of relative monads and relative adjunctions in a virtual equipment, extending the theory of monads and adjunctions in a 2-category. The theory of relative comonads and relative coadjunctions follows by duality. While some aspects of the theory behave analogously to the non-relative setting, others require new insights. In particular, the universal properties that define the algebra object and the opalgebra object for a monad qua trivial relative monad are stronger than the classical notions of algebra object and opalgebra object for a monad qua monad. Inter alia, we prove a number of representation theorems for relative monads, establishing the unity of several concepts in the literature, including the devices of Walters, the j-monads of Diers, and the relative monads of Altenkirch, Chapman, and Uustalu. A motivating setting is the virtual equipment \mathbb{V} \text{-} \mathbf{Cat} of categories enriched in a monoidal category \mathbb{V}, though many of our results are new even for \mathbb{V} = \mathbf{Set}.

frontmatter.title: Left- and right-cocartesian 2-cells in a virtual bicategory

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{K} be a virtual bicategory; given a path { A } \xrightarrow{\Psi} { B } \in \mathcal{K} ^{ \star } and an edge { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B }, a 2-cell { \Psi } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { f } is called left-cocartesian if for 2-cell { \Psi ; \Xi } \xrightarrow{\beta} { s } factors uniquely through \alpha in the sense that there exists a unique 2-cell { { \left [ f \right ]} ; \Psi } \xrightarrow{\beta \setminus \alpha} { s } such that \beta = { \left [ \alpha , \overrightarrow{1 _{ \Xi _i } \ldots} \right ]} ; { \left ( \beta \setminus \alpha \right )}.

Dually, given a path { B } \xrightarrow{\Psi} { C } \in \mathcal{K} ^{ \star } and an edge { B } \xrightarrow{f} { C }, a 2-cell { \Psi } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { f } is called right-cocartesian if for 2-cell { \Phi ; \Psi } \xrightarrow{\beta} { s } factors uniquely through \alpha in the sense that there exists a unique 2-cell { \Phi ; { \left [ f \right ]} } \xrightarrow{\beta \setminus \alpha} { s } such that \beta = { \left [ \overrightarrow{1 _{ \Phi _i } \ldots} , \alpha \right ]} ; { \left ( \beta \setminus \alpha \right )}.
These definitions are due to Arkor and McDermott.
